titleStmt.title: First-Principles Calculation of Superconducting T c in Superhard B-C-N Metals

analytic.title: First-Principles Calculation of Superconducting T c in Superhard B-C-N Metals

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We perform first-principles electron-phonon calculations to evaluate the superconducting transition temperature Tc for ternary superhard metals B2C3N and B4C5N3. These materials are predicted to exhibit a wide distribution of electron-phonon coupling parameters on their multiple Fermi surface sheets, which necessitates solving the anisotropic Eliashberg equations for accurate determination of Tc. An ambient-pressure Tc of ∼ 40 K and ∼ 20 K is obtained respectively for B2C3N and B4C5N3 from the anisotropic gap equations. The relatively high Tc of these compounds is due in part to their high Debye temperatures associated with superhardness. The materials under study are potentially synthesizable, as their formation energies are comparable to those of other recently synthesized superhard B-C-N compounds. Therefore, studying superhard metals could hold the promise of realizing new higher-Tc superconductors at ambient pressure.

Introduction -A recent resurgence of interests in studying phonon-mediated Bardeen-Coopper-Schrieffer (BCS) superconductors has largely resulted from the successful prediction and realization of superconducting high-pressure hydrides H 3 S and LaH 10 [1][2][3][4][5]. These achievements have greatly enhanced the prospects of room-temperature superconductivity. However, the requirement for high external pressures in the megabar (Mbar or 100 GPa) range to synthesize and maintain superconducting states in these materials limits their practical applications. In addition to hydrogen-rich materials [6][7][8], other high-pressure systems based on light elements beryllium (Be), boron (B), carbon (C), and/or nitrogen (N) also have been studied [9][10][11][12][13][14][15]. Searching for new, synthesizable superconductors with higher T c at reduced pressure would promise to revolutionize the energy, transportation, and information technologies [16][17][18].

Generally, the T c of phonon-mediated (BCS) superconductors increases in materials with a large electronphonon coupling strength λ, and/or a high Debye temperature Θ D [19,20]. Recent theoretical studies have suggested that T c ≤ AΘ D for BCS superconductors, with the pre-factor A ∼ 0.1 [21,22]. Θ D is typically elevated in highly compressed materials due to pressure-induced hardening. In the absence of external pressure, high Θ D can occur in materials with superior mechanical properties, such as those exhibiting superhardness. In Vickers indentation test [23], hardness H V is determined by the ratio of the applied force to the resulting indentation area. A material is called superhard when its H V ≥ 40 GPa [24]. At ambient pressure, diamond is the known hardest material with H V ∼ 100 GPa [25] and a high Θ D ∼ 2200 K [26]. However, diamond fails to become a BCS superconductor due to its large insulating gap. On the other hand, a metallic superhard compound, BC 5 , was theoretically suggested to show a relatively high T c in the range ∼ 11 -45 K, depending on the underlying crystal structures [27][28][29]. BC 5 with a local covalentlybonded structure similar to cubic diamond can exhibit a high hardness H V ≥ 60 GPa [30,31]. The T c for other binary superhard compounds like BC 3 and BC 7 were also computed to be between ∼ 11 -23 K [32,33]. It remains an important experimental task to verify if these materials can be synthesized in the proposed structures, and if they can superconduct at the high theoretical T c .

In this work, we perform first-principles electronphonon calculations to study the superconducting T c of B 2 C 3 N and B 4 C 5 N 3 , which were recently predicted to be superhard metals (with H V ≥ 40 GPa) by machine learning and evolutionary structure searches [34]. These materials exhibit multiple nested Fermi surface sheets, which suggests anisotropic electron-phonon couplings and necessitates solutions of the anisotropic Eliashberg equations for a precise determination of T c . The anisotropic calculations indicate T c values of ∼ 40 K and ∼ 20 K for B 2 C 3 N and B 4 C 5 N 3 , respectively. Notably, the theoretical formation energy of B 2 C 3 N and B 4 C 5 N 3 is comparable to that of previously synthesized ternary superhard materials such as BC 2 N and BC 10 N [35,36]. Therefore, it is feasible that the B-C-N materials under study could be synthesized by high-pressure high-temperature (HPHT) techniques or microwave plasma chemical vapor deposition (MPCVD). Consequently, superhard metals could offer an intriguing new avenue for achieving higher-T c BCS superconductors under ambient pressure.

Methods -First-principles density functional theory (DFT) calculations are based on the Quantum ESPRESSO software (version 7.2) [37,38], and electronphonon calculations are performed with the EPW code (version 5.7) [39][40][41]. In addition to B 2 C 3 N and B 4 C 5 N 3 , we perform benchmark calculations for BC 5 and MgB 2 (the current T c record-holder among ambient-pressure BCS superconductors). Figure 1 shows the structures under study. The BC 5 , B 2 C 3 N, and B 4 C 5 N 3 structures are sourced from their respective papers [27,34] and arranged into 12-atom unit cells. The structure for MgB 2 is sourced from the Materials Project [42]. The Monkhorst-Pack sampling scheme [43] is used with a Γ-centered k-point mesh of resolution 0.02 × 2π Å-1 along each reciprocal lattice vector. A Marzari-Vanderbilt smear- ing [44] of 6 mRy is employed. The convergence criteria of self-consistent and structural relaxation calculations are set to 10 -8 Ry per unit cell and 10 -6 Ry per Bohr radius, respectively. We adopt a wavefunction kinetic energy cutoff of 100 Ry, which suffices to converge the DFT total energy difference within 10 -6 Ry per atom. For each crystal structure, we fully relax the lattice parameters and atomic positions without pressure. Afterwards, we compute the electronic, phonon, and electronphonon properties. All calculations use norm-conserving pseudopotentials from the Pseudo Dojo library [45] and the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange-correlation functional [46]. The dynamical matrices and linear variation of the self-consistent potential are calculated with density-functional perturbation theory (DFPT) [47].

The EPW calculations use Wannier interpolation on uniform Γ-centered k-grids with the Wannier90 code [48,49]. In the cases of BC 5 , B 2 C 3 N, and B 4 C 5 N 3 , we consider 2p orbitals for every B and C atom as initial projections for the maximally localized Wannier functions [50]; in MgB 2 , Mg 3s and B 2p z orbitals are considered. After Wannierization, we perform electron-phonon computation on dense 70×70×14 (70×70×70) k and 35×35 ×7 (35×35×35) Fourier-interpolated q grids for the B-C and B-C-N systems (MgB 2 ) to evaluate the Eliashberg spectral function α 2 F (ω) and the isotropic electron-phonon coupling λ. The anisotropic Eliashberg equations are solved on the same dense interpolated k and q grids with imaginary Matsubara frequencies [51]. The solutions are brought to the real axis with Padé approximants. The crystal structures and Fermi surfaces are visualized respectively by VESTA [52] and FermiSurfer [53] softwares.
Results and Discussion -Figure 2 shows the electronic band structures and density of states (DOS). BC The electronic structure for MgB 2 is shown in Fig.

div.head: 2(d).
div.p: There is a notable difference in the band topology between MgB 2 and the other compounds under study. MgB 2 is known to exhibit a complex mixture of σ and π orbitals, which manifest as both 2D and 3D Fermi surface sheets [54]. Conversely, p orbitals dominate the bands near the E F in BC 5 , B 2 C 3 N, and B 4 C 5 N 3 . The variation in N F suggests differences in how electrons interact with phonons in these materials, which play a vital role in determining their phonon-mediated superconductivity.

Figure 3 left panels show phonon dispersions along high-symmetry paths of the materials under study. The absence of negative phonon modes confirms the dynamic stability of all the materials at ambient pressure. As seen in Figs. 3(a)-(c), the phonon spectra of BC 5 , B 2 C 3 N, and B 4 C 5 N 3 exhibit a prominent contribution from highfrequency optical phonons, which is a common feature of covalently bonded superhard materials like diamond. In contrast, MgB 2 displays a smaller phonon bandwidth and a quite different band topology, as seen in Fig. 3

div.head: (d).
div.p: There is also a major difference in the distribution of the mode-resolved electron-phonon coupling λ qν [39][40][41]:
div.formula: λ qν = ℏ 2N F mn,k W k 1 ω 2 qν |g mn,ν | 2 δ(ϵ nk )δ(ϵ mk+q ),(1)
div.p: where W k is a weight factor equivalent to the reciprocal number of k points on the Fermi surface; ω qν is the phonon energy of mode ν at phonon wavevector q; g mn,ν is the electron-phonon coupling matrix element between electron bands m and n with phonon mode index ν; ϵ nk and ϵ mk+q are the electron eigen-energies with band indices n and m at momenta k and k + q, respectively. As seen in the purple shaded areas of Figs. 3(a)-(c), λ qν in the B-C and B-C-N systems predominantly arises from the higher-energy optical phonons around 90 meV and 120 meV. There are additional contributions to λ qν from the lower-energy acoustic phonons near 50 meV. These low-energy contributions are associated with notable phonon softenings and enhanced λ qν along the K -Γ and H -A Brillouin zone paths. As seen later in Fig. 4 insets, the Fermi surface topologies of the materials under study provide good nesting conditions, which can induce phonon softenings and Kohn anomalies to facilitate a stronger electron-phonon coupling [55][56][57] As a The mode-resolved electron-phonon coupling λ qν (already averaged over k) can be further averaged over q to obtain the isotropic electron-phonon coupling λ:
div.formula: λ = 1 N q qν λ qν = 2 α 2 F (ω) ω dω.(2)
div.p: Here, N q is the total number of points in the fine q grid. α 2 F (ω) is the Eliashberg spectral function defined as:
div.formula: α 2 F (ω) = 1 2N q qν ω qν λ qν δ(ω -ω qν ). (3
div.p: The phonon density of states (PHDOS), α 2 F (ω), and λ are plotted respectively as blue, green, and purple curves in the right panels of Fig. 3. Compared to MgB 2 , BC 5 and B 2 C 3 N achieve a comparable λ 0.7 due to contributions from high-energy phonon modes, which compensate for their lower electron DOS at the E F (N F ).

The lattice stiffness and superhardness of the B-C and B-C-N compounds play crucial roles in sustaining strong electron-phonon coupling at these higher phonon energies. On the other hand, B 4 C 5 N 3 exhibits a slightly smaller λ ∼ 0.6, likely due to an even smaller N F . Often times, it suffices to estimate the T c of phononmediated superconductors by Allen-Dynes equation [20]:
The logarithmic average frequency ω log is defined as:
div.formula: ω log = exp 2 λ ∞ 0 dω α 2 F (ω) ω log(ω) . (5
div.p: The Coulomb pseudopotential µ * entering the Allen-Dynes equation is an effective parameter describing the effects of Coulomb repulsion on superconductivity. µ * is found to range between 0.1 and 0.2 for many BCS superconductors. We use µ * = 0.1 for all the systems under study. As shown later, solutions of the anisotropic Eliashberg gap equations for MgB 2 using µ * = 0.1 lead to a T c ∼ 40 K in good agreement with the experiments. Table I summarizes the calculated λ, ω log , and the superconducting transition temperature T c,AD using the Allen-Dynes equation (Eq. 4) with µ * = 0.1. BC 5 achieves a much higher T c,AD compared to the other compounds under study. However, the isotropic approximation of λ does not account for the anisotropy in electronphonon interactions. The presence of multiple nested Fermi surface sheets (Fig. 4 insets) in the B-C and B-C-N compounds, as well as in MgB 2 , also suggests that their superconducting gaps may be highly anisotropic. Additionally, the computed T c,AD for MgB 2 is only 22.5 K, much lower than the experimental value of ∼ 40 K. This discrepancy strongly suggests that solutions of the full anisotropic Eliashberg gap equations are required. The anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg (aM E) theory using Wannier functions [51] takes into account momentum-dependent interactions on the Fermi surface and variations in electron-phonon coupling across different phonon modes. By solving the aM E equations on fine momentum and frequency grids, we can determine the anisotropic superconducting gaps on the Fermi surface to more accurately evaluate T c . Figure 4 The superconducting T c of the aM E theory can be obtained by fitting the leading edge of the superconducting gap on the Fermi surface as a function of temperature, ∆ k (T ), to the analytical BCS gap equation:

The fitted superconducting transition temperatures T c,aM E are summarized in Table I. In particular, T c,aM E for MgB 2 is 40.7 K, which agrees well with the experiments. BC 5 and B 2 C 3 N also show a T c,aM E ∼ 40 K comparable to that of MgB 2 . Compared to the Allen-Dynes T c,AD under an isotropic approximation, our results indicate the importance of investigating the full anisotorpic Eliashberg gap equations for accurate T c determination.

Finally, the anisotropic nature of the materials under study also can be seen from the distribution ρ(λ) of the momentum-resolved electron-phonon coupling on the Fermi surface λ k , as shown in the right panels of Fig. (c)-(d)], the anisotropy is even more substantial due to the presence of two distinct superconducting gaps. In each gap, λ k is fairly anisotropic with a wide distribution. Such an anisotropy in λ k leads to a highly underestimated T c in the isotropic approximation of the electron-phonon coupling.

Conclusion -We have performed detailed firstprinciples electron-phonon calculations to study superhard metals BC 5 , B 2 C 3 N, and B 4 C 5 N 3 . Their superconducting transition temperatures computed using anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg theory were found comparable to that of MgB 2 , which, two decades after its discovery, remains the T c record holder among ambient- pressure BCS superconductors. The B-C-N materials under study have relatively low formation energies and can potentially be made by high-pressure high-temperature (HPHT) synthesis or microwave plasma chemical vapor deposition (MPCVD). Further theoretical explorations of T c in different superhard metals via the anisotropic Eliashberg gap equations, alongside experimental investigations of their synthesizability and superconducting properties, are important future research areas for discovering new higher-T c phonon-mediated superconductors.

figure.head: FIG. 1 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 1. Schematic crystal structures under study. BC5, B2C3N, and B4C5N3 with a 12-atom unit cell belong to trigonal symmetry of space group P3m1 (No. 156). MgB2 belongs to hexagonal symmetry of space group P6/mmm (No. 191).

5 , B 2 C 3 N, and B 4 C 5 N 3 all exhibit local covalently bonded atomic arrangements similar to diamond. Therefore, they have similar band structures [Figs. 2(a)-(c)], but are effectively hole doped with the Fermi level (E F ) residing within the valence bands. Specifically, BC 5 and B 2 C 3 N feature five bands crossing the E F with comparable electron DOS at the Fermi level (N F ). In contrast, B 4 C 5 N 3 shows only three bands crossing the E F with a much lower N F . This variation in N F between B 2 C 3 N and B 4 C 5 N 3 is expected, due to the differing hole doping levels based on the valence electron counts of B and N atoms. A larger difference in the B-to-N ratio in the chemical composition will result in stronger hole doping.

figure.head: FIG. 2 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 2. Electronic band structures along high-symmetry paths [left] and density of states [right] for (a) BC5, (b) B2C3N, (c) B4C5N3, and (d) MgB2. The energy is plotted relative to the Fermi level (EF ) indicated by the horizontal dashed line. The B-C and B-C-N compounds exhibit band structures similar to diamond but are effectively hole doped.

figure.head: FIG. 3 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 3. Phonon dispersions along high-symmetry paths [blue curves, left panels] for (a) BC5, (b) B2C3N, (c) B4C5N3, and (d) MgB2. A Gaussian-filtered average of mode-resolved electron-phonon coupling λqν is plotted in purple, with the width displaying its intensity. The right panels show the phonon density of states (PHDOS), Eliashberg spectral function α 2 F (ω), and isotropic electron-phonon coupling λ.

left panels show the resulting anisotropic gap functions and the Fermi surfaces of the materials under study. BC 5 and B 2 C 3 N exhibit a similar Fermi surface topology and a single superconducting gap [Figs. 4(a)-(b)]. In contrast, B 4 C 5 N 3 manifests two gaps similar to MgB 2 [Figs. 4(c)-(d)]. B 4 C 5 N 3 has a Fermi surface structure similar to BC 5 and B 2 C 3 N, but with a flatter and more pronounced hexagonal central band and cylindrical outer band. In MgB 2 , the two gaps originate from the distinct 2D and 3D Fermi surfaces. Its σ bands are quasi-two-dimensional and show stronger electron-phonon coupling with lowenergy phonon modes, causing a higher superconducting gap. The π bands are more three-dimensional, showing a weaker coupling and a lower superconducting gap.

The width of ρ(λ) indicates the degree of anisotropy. In BC 5 and B 2 C 3 N [Figs. 4(a)-(b)], while λ k is peaked around 0.7 and 0.8, its distribution spans from 0.5 to 1.0. In B 4 C 5 N 3 and MgB 2 [Figs. 4

figure.head: FIG. 4 .
figure.figDesc: FIG. 4. Superconducting gap ∆ k as a function of temperature [green curves, left panels] computed by anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg theory for (a) BC5, (b) B2C3N, (c) B4C5N3, and (d) MgB2. The insets show the Fermi surfaces. The blue dots are first-moment averages of ∆ k , and they are fitted to the BCS gap equation (black dashed line) to determine the Tc. The right panels show the distribution ρ(λ) of the momentumresolved electron-phonon coupling on the Fermi surface λ k .

figure.head: TABLE I .
figure.label: I
figure.figDesc: First-principles results for the electron density of states at the Fermi level NF (in units of states/atom), the dimensionless isotropic electron-phonon coupling λ, the logarithmic average frequency ω log (in meV), the superconducting transition temperature computed by the Allen-Dynes equation Tc,AD (in Kelvin), and the superconducting transition temperature Tc,aME (in Kelvin) obtained by fitting the BCS gap equation to solutions of the anisotropic Migdal-Eliashberg theory. Both Tc,AD and Tc,aME are calculated with µ

div.p: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS The research is supported by the U.S. National Science Foundation (NSF) Award No. DMR-2310526. C.-C.C. also acknowledges support from NSF Award No. DMR-2142801. The calculations utilized the Frontera computing system at the Texas Advanced Computing Center made possible by NSF Award No. OAC-1818253.
